title: Augmented Human Development Index vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: The Augmented Human Development Index (AHDI) is a summary measure of achievement in four key dimensions of human development: a long and healthy life, being knowledgeable, being free and having a decent standard of living. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Augmented Human Development Index vs. GDP per capita

The Augmented Human Development Index (AHDI) is a summary measure of achievement in four key dimensions of human development: a long and healthy life, being knowledgeable, being free and having a decent standard of living. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot displays the relationship between the Augmented Human Development Index (AHDI) on the vertical axis and GDP per capita on the horizontal axis for the year 2020. The horizontal axis uses a logarithmic scale to represent GDP per capita in international dollars at 2011 prices, ranging from $1,000 to over $100,000. The vertical axis measures the AHDI score from 0 to roughly 0.9. Data points are color-coded by region, with Europe and North America generally occupying the upper-right quadrant, indicating high scores in both metrics. Countries such as Switzerland, the United States, and Japan cluster with AHDI values above 0.8 and GDP per capita exceeding $40,000. In contrast, nations in Africa, including Burundi, Chad, and the DR Congo, are grouped in the lower-left area, with AHDI scores below 0.3 and GDP per capita under $2,000. A positive correlation is visible across the dataset, where higher income levels typically align with higher development indices. Circle sizes correspond to population, making large countries like India, China, and Indonesia visually prominent in the middle range of the chart. Specific outliers include Qatar, which has a very high GDP per capita near $100,000 but an AHDI score around 0.4, and Equatorial Guinea, which sits lower on the development index relative to its income level compared to European peers.
